skill.name: faers-multi-drug-soc-planner
skill.description: Generates complete FAERS-based multi-drug single-SOC safety comparison research designs from a user-provided drug set, comparator, and adverse event domain. Always use this skill when users want to compare safety signals across multiple drugs using FAERS or OpenFDA data within one System Organ Class (SOC) or bounded AE domain. Trigger for:"FAERS study comparing drugs within one SOC", "publishable FAERS safety comparison paper", "compare neuropsychiatric adverse events across beta-blockers", "Lite/Standard/Advanced FAERS safety plans", "active-comparator restricted disproportionality", "adjusted ROR logistic regression FAERS", "within-class head-to-head drug comparison", "pharmacovigilance signal comparison", "single-SOC PT-level FAERS design", or any phrasing like "I want to compare drug X and drug Y for adverse events in FAERS" or "build a comparative pharmacovigilance paper". Always output four workload configurations (Lite / Standard / Advanced / Publication+) with a recommended primary plan, step-by-step workflow, figure plan, validation strategy, minimal executable version, and publication upgrade path.
skill.summary: Generates complete FAERS-based multi-drug single-SOC safety comparison research designs from a user-provided drug set, comparator, and adverse event domain.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 55 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 43 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are drug names (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, lisinopril, propranolol) and medical terminology misidentified by pattern matching. The 8 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown backtick formatting for interface variable names like `drug_set` and `comparator`. The 4 'Hardcoded URL' findings are legitimate references to the GitHub source repository and the FDA FAERS data download page. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was detected. This skill generates structured study design text plans only and contains no executable code, shell scripts, or network-dependent runtime behavior.

low_findings.title: Drug Names Misidentified as Cryptographic Algorithms
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged drug names (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, lisinopril, propranolol, metoprolol, atenolol) and medical terminology throughout both files as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. These are standard pharmacovigilance and pharmacoepidemiology terms in a medical research skill - not cryptography references.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged markdown inline code formatting backticks in SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. Lines 30-33 use backticks to format interface variable names (`drug_set`, `comparator`, `target_soc`, `config_preference`). Line 103 uses backticks to format role-code abbreviations (`PS`, `SS`, `C`, `I`). These are standard markdown formatting, not shell commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Legitimate References
low_findings.description: The two flagged URLs are legitimate source and data references: (1) line 7 links to the GitHub source repository for the skill, and (2) line 96 links to the official FDA FAERS data download page (fis.fda.gov). These are not malicious or unexpected - they are required references for a pharmacovigilance research design skill that describes how to access FDA data.

low_findings.title: Drug Classification Text Misidentified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The flagging of 'System reconnaissance' and 'Network reconnaissance' is a false positive triggered by pharmacologic drug classification terminology. Line 145 of the eval report discusses corticosteroid co-use in fluoroquinolone safety analysis. Line 20 of SKILL.md discusses lipophilic versus hydrophilic drug property classification. These are standard pharmacoepidemiology terms, not reconnaissance activity.

low_findings.title: Text in JSON Field Misidentified as Python Library Import
low_findings.description: The 'Python HTTP libraries' flag at line 57 of the eval report refers to evaluation text within a JSON field, not an actual Python import statement. The eval report is a JSON data file, not executable code. No Python libraries are imported anywhere in this skill.

content.user_title: Plan Multi-Drug Safety Comparison Studies with FAERS
content.value_statement: Designing comparative pharmacovigilance studies requires careful methodological planning across drug normalization, comparator selection, and statistical analysis. This skill generates complete multi-drug safety comparison research designs from a simple drug set, comparator, and target adverse event domain.
actual_capabilities: Generates study designs at four workload levels: Lite, Standard, Advanced, and Publication+
Creates active-comparator restricted disproportionality analysis plans with adjusted ROR
Builds within-class head-to-head drug comparison frameworks with multiplicity correction
Produces publication-ready figure and table plans for pharmacovigilance papers
Generates step-by-step workflows with data quality gates and validation strategies
Provides minimal executable versions with 2-3 week timelines for rapid pilot studies
limitations: Does not execute FAERS data queries or perform statistical analysis
Limited to single-SOC study designs; does not generate all-SOC sweeping analyses
Requires user familiarity with pharmacovigilance and disproportionality concepts
Cannot validate FAERS data quality or address underreporting bias

use_cases.title: Pharmacovigilance Research Planning
use_cases.description: Design a publishable safety comparison study within one System Organ Class, with options for various statistical methods and publication tiers.

use_cases.title: Drug Safety Signal Assessment
use_cases.description: Compare safety profiles of drugs within the same therapeutic class using FAERS disproportionality analysis with active-comparator control.

use_cases.title: Publication-Focused Study Design
use_cases.description: Generate publication-ready study protocols including sensitivity analysis, pharmacologic subgroup comparisons, and reviewer defense sections.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Drug Class Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare beta-blockers vs ACE inhibitors for psychiatric adverse events in FAERS. Give me all four configurations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new pharmacovigilance comparison with minimal inputs

prompt_templates.title: Head-to-Head Within-Class Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a head-to-head safety study comparing propranolol, atenolol, and metoprolol for neuropsychiatric adverse events using OpenFDA data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing multiple drugs within the same therapeutic class

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Adjusted ROR Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a FAERS study comparing GLP-1 receptor agonists vs DPP-4 inhibitors for gastrointestinal adverse events with active-comparator restriction and logistic regression adjustment.

prompt_templates.title: Full Publication-Grade Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a publication-ready comparative pharmacovigilance protocol for fluoroquinolones vs beta-lactams for tendon adverse events. Include pharmacologic subgroup comparison, sensitivity analysis, and a minimal executable 3-week version.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a high-impact journal submission with comprehensive methodology

output_examples.input: Compare beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol, metoprolol) vs lisinopril for psychiatric adverse events in FAERS.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a structured study plan with four workload configurations: Lite (2-4 weeks, crude plus adjusted ROR), Standard (5-8 weeks, full active-comparator design with PT deepening), Advanced (8-13 weeks, adds pharmacologic subgroup comparison), and Publication+ (12-18 weeks, adds alternate comparator robustness). It recommends the Standard plan as primary due to balanced rigor and timeline, and includes a detailed workflow covering drug normalization, comparator definition, SOC and PT outcome definition, descriptive characterization, logistic regression, and sensitivity analysis.

output_examples.input: I need a FAERS study comparing fluoroquinolones vs beta-lactams for tendon adverse events. Minimal plan only.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a Minimal Executable Version focused on OpenFDA data access, one drug class versus one active comparator, primary suspect restriction, drug name normalization, crude ROR calculation, 3 to 5 key PTs, one summary table, and one forest plot. It includes a Data Quality Gate requiring 30 or more cases per drug group, a fallback to quarterly ASCII downloads if the OpenFDA API is unavailable, and flags fluoroquinolone-corticosteroid interaction risk as a known confounder.

best_practices: Provide a specific drug set, active comparator, and target SOC for the most focused study design
Use the recommended primary plan as your starting point before customizing for available resources
Review the validation and robustness plan section to understand study limitations before execution
anti_patterns: Requesting an all-SOC sweep or pan-MedDRA signal scan instead of a focused single-SOC design
Treating FAERS disproportionality signals (ROR or PRR) as causal evidence of drug safety risks
Omitting comparator logic and depending entirely on automated inference for study design

faq.question: What is FAERS?
faq.answer: FAERS is the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System, a database of adverse event reports submitted to the FDA. It is used for pharmacovigilance signal detection and safety monitoring.

faq.question: What is a System Organ Class (SOC)?
faq.answer: A SOC is a high-level medical category in the MedDRA terminology system, such as Psychiatric disorders or Cardiac disorders. This skill designs studies within one specific SOC.

faq.question: Can this skill run statistical analyses?
faq.answer: No. This skill generates study design plans only. You must execute the analyses using R, Python, or other statistical software.

faq.question: What is ROR in pharmacovigilance?
faq.answer: ROR (Reporting Odds Ratio) is a disproportionality measure that compares the proportion of a specific adverse event reported for a drug versus all other drugs in the database.

faq.question: What is active-comparator restricted disproportionality?
faq.answer: A method that restricts the analysis to drugs with the same therapeutic indication, reducing confounding by indication compared to using all other drugs as the comparator group.

faq.question: Does this skill work with OpenFDA data?
faq.answer: Yes. The study plans include options for OpenFDA API access and FAERS quarterly ASCII file downloads. The Lite and Minimal configurations use OpenFDA only.
